Day 1: How the Web Actually Works — From Browser to Server
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I’m revisiting web development from scratch to understand the “why” behind every concept. Today’s lesson explores browsers, DNS, IP addresses, servers, caching, and how a website reaches your screen.
For the past few years, I’ve been working with React and building web applications. But recently, I realised something important.
I knew how to build applications, but I didn’t always understand why they worked the way they did.
Instead of jumping straight into another React course, I’ve decided to restart my software engineering journey from the very beginning. My goal isn’t to memorise syntax — it’s to understand the fundamentals that every modern web application is built upon.
They say it’s better late than never. I believe it’s never too late to go back, strengthen your fundamentals, and become a better engineer. That’s exactly what I’m setting out to do.
So, This is Day 1 of that journey.
I started with one simple question:
What actually happens when we type google.com into a browser and press Enter?
That one question opened the door to understanding how the web really works.

🌐 What Is a Browser?
Most of us use browsers every day, but we rarely think about what they actually do.
A browser is much more than an application used to open websites.
It:

• Sends requests to servers.

• Receives responses.

• Reads HTML.

• Applies CSS.

• Executes JavaScript.

• Stores cache.

• Manages cookies.

• Displays everything as a webpage that we can interact with.
Some common browsers are Google Chrome, Microsoft Edge, Safari, and Firefox.
You can think of the browser as a translator between humans and computers.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

💻 What Is a Website?
A website is simply a collection of files stored on another computer somewhere on the internet.
These files can include:

• HTML

• CSS

• JavaScript

• Images

• Videos

• Fonts
Whenever we open a website, we’re actually requesting those files from another computer.

🤝 Client vs Server
This was one of my favourite concepts.
The client is the application that sends the request.
Examples:

• Chrome Browser

• Safari

• Instagram App
The server is another computer whose job is to store data and respond to requests from clients.
A simple way to remember it is:
User → Client (Browser/App) → Server

📍 What Is an IP Address?
Every device connected to the internet has a unique numerical address called an IP Address.
Computers communicate using IP addresses — not names.
For example, while we remember a website as:
google.com
computers actually communicate using something like:
142.xxx.xxx.xxx
Imagine remembering numbers like these for every website you visit. It would be nearly impossible.
That’s exactly why DNS exists.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

🌍 What Is DNS?
DNS stands for Domain Name System.
You can think of it as the internet’s phonebook.
Its job is to translate a human-friendly domain name like:
google.com
into the corresponding IP address that computers understand.
One thing I found interesting is that DNS does not store website content.
It only stores the mapping between:
Domain Name → IP Address
The actual website content still lives on the server.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

🔄 What Happens When You Open a Website?
Here’s the complete flow that I learned today:
You
⬇️
Browser
⬇️
DNS
⬇️
IP Address
⬇️
Server
⬇️
HTML
⬇️
Browser
⬇️
Website
Breaking it into small steps made the entire process much easier to understand.

📦 Browser Cache
One question that really made me think was:
Why does a website sometimes still open even after turning off Wi-Fi?
The answer is browser caching.
Browsers can store previously downloaded resources like HTML (depending on caching rules), CSS, JavaScript, images, and fonts. If those cached resources are still valid, the browser can reuse them instead of downloading them again.
However, dynamic information like new messages, notifications, or the latest posts usually requires a fresh request to the server.

💡 My Biggest Takeaway
The biggest realisation I had today was this:
The browser doesn’t magically know everything.
When we type a website address, it doesn’t already know where that website lives.
It first asks the DNS system for the IP address.
Only after getting the IP address can it communicate with the correct server and request the website.
Understanding this flow changed the way I look at web development.

🚀 What’s Next?
In Day 2, I’ll be learning:

• What is HTTP?

• What is HTTPS?

• What are Requests and Responses?

• Why do APIs exist?

• How browsers actually communicate with servers.
I’m documenting this journey not because I’m an expert, but because I believe the best way to truly learn something is to understand it well enough to explain it in simple words.
If you’re also revisiting the fundamentals or just starting your web development journey, I’d love for you to learn alongside me.
If you have any questions, suggestions, or feedback, feel free to email me at urbashijrall3003@gmail.com
